description: Core computer science and software engineering concepts for technical interviews

topics.concept: Data Structures — Arrays vs Linked Lists
topics.explanation: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory, giving O(1) random access but O(n) insertion/deletion. Linked lists use nodes with pointers, giving O(1) insertion at head but O(n) access. Choose arrays for read-heavy workloads and linked lists for frequent insertions.
sample_questions: When would you choose a linked list over an array?
What is the time complexity of accessing the k-th element in each?

topics.explanation: Stack follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) — used for function call stacks, undo operations, DFS. Queue follows FIFO (First In, First Out) — used for BFS, task scheduling, message queues.
sample_questions: Explain the difference between a stack and a queue.
Give a real-world use case for each.

topics.explanation: Hash maps provide O(1) average-case for insert, delete, and lookup via hash functions. Collisions are resolved by chaining (linked lists at each bucket) or open addressing. Load factor determines when to resize.
sample_questions: How do hash maps handle collisions?
What is a load factor and why does it matter?

topics.explanation: Big-O describes the worst-case growth rate of an algorithm. Common complexities: O(1) constant, O(log n) binary search, O(n) linear scan, O(n log n) merge sort, O(n²) nested loops. Space complexity follows the same notation.
sample_questions: What is the time complexity of binary search?
How would you determine the time complexity of nested loops?

topics.explanation: Five OOP design principles: Single Responsibility (one reason to change), Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification), Liskov Substitution (subtypes replaceable for supertypes), Interface Segregation (small focused interfaces), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concretions).
sample_questions: Explain the Single Responsibility Principle with a code example.
How does the Dependency Inversion Principle enable testability?

topics.explanation: REST uses HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) over stateless resources identified by URLs. Key principles: statelessness, uniform interface, resource-based URIs, proper status codes (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500). Versioning via URL path or header.
sample_questions: What HTTP status codes would you use for a resource not found vs unauthorized?
How do you version a REST API?

topics.explanation: SQL databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL) are relational, ACID-compliant, schema-enforced, and suited for complex queries. NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis) trade ACID for scalability, schema flexibility, and specialised access patterns. Choose based on consistency requirements and query patterns.
sample_questions: When would you choose a NoSQL database over a relational one?
What does ACID stand for and why does it matter?

topics.explanation: Cache-aside (lazy loading): app checks cache, falls back to DB. Write-through: writes go to cache and DB simultaneously. Write-back: writes go to cache first, DB asynchronously. Cache eviction policies: LRU, LFU, TTL. Redis is the standard in-memory cache.
sample_questions: Explain cache-aside vs write-through patterns.
How do you handle cache invalidation?

topics.explanation: Decompose a monolith into small, independently deployable services each owning its data store. Benefits: independent scaling, technology diversity, fault isolation. Challenges: network latency, distributed transactions, service discovery, operational complexity.
sample_questions: What are the main challenges of moving from a monolith to microservices?
How do you handle a transaction that spans multiple microservices?

topics.explanation: A distributed system can guarantee at most 2 of 3 properties: Consistency (all nodes see same data), Availability (every request gets a response), Partition Tolerance (system works despite network splits). Since partitions are unavoidable, systems trade C vs A. CP: HBase, Zookeeper. AP: Cassandra, CouchDB.
sample_questions: Explain the CAP theorem with a practical example.
When would you choose AP over CP?

topics.explanation: Message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS) decouple producers from consumers, enabling async processing and load levelling. Kafka provides durable, ordered, replayable event streams. Event-driven architecture enables loose coupling but complicates tracing and debugging.
sample_questions: When would you use Kafka over RabbitMQ?
How does event sourcing differ from traditional CRUD?

topics.explanation: Continuous Integration merges code frequently with automated testing. Continuous Delivery ensures code is always releasable. Continuous Deployment auto-deploys every passing build. Key tools: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI. Best practices: fast feedback, trunk-based development, feature flags.
sample_questions: What is the difference between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment?
How would you structure a CI pipeline for a microservices project?

topics.explanation: Core components: ID generation (base62 encoding of auto-increment or UUID), key-value store (Redis + DynamoDB), redirect (HTTP 301 vs 302), analytics (click tracking via async events). Scale: read-heavy, cache popular URLs, CDN for global latency. Handle hash collisions with retry logic.
sample_questions: Design a URL shortener that handles 100 million redirects per day.
How would you ensure short code uniqueness at scale?

topics.explanation: Race condition: two threads access shared data concurrently with at least one write. Mutex (mutual exclusion lock) serialises access. Deadlock: two threads each hold a resource the other needs. Prevention: lock ordering, timeouts, lock-free data structures. Go channels and Python asyncio avoid shared-state concurrency.
sample_questions: What is a race condition and how do you prevent it?
How would you detect and resolve a deadlock?

topics.explanation: Observer: defines one-to-many dependency (event listeners, Redux store). Factory: encapsulates object creation logic. Singleton: ensures a single instance (often an anti-pattern in tests). Strategy: swappable algorithms at runtime. Prefer composition over inheritance.
sample_questions: Where would you apply the Observer pattern?
Why is Singleton often considered an anti-pattern?
